title: Why your body does not believe your affirmations
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
You are not broken. Your nervous system is just doing its job.

htmlContent:
You are not broken. Your nervous system is just doing its job.

I used to stand in front of a mirror every morning and say, "I am safe. I am worthy. I am enough." I said it with conviction. I said it with feeling. And for about thirty seconds, I almost believed it. Then I'd walk into the kitchen, my husband would clear his throat, and my stomach would drop. My chest would tighten. My throat would close. And that affirmation? It evaporated like it never existed.

Here's the thing. Your body does not believe your affirmations. And it's not because you're doing them wrong. It's not because you're not trying hard enough. It's not because you're broken or resistant or somehow failing at healing. It's because your nervous system has its own language. And that language is not words.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system processes safety and threat. He found that the body's threat detection system operates below conscious awareness. It doesn't listen to what you say. It listens to what it senses. Tone of voice. Facial expression. Heart rate. Muscle tension. The smell of someone's cologne that reminds your body of a person who hurt you. Your nervous system is scanning for cues of safety or danger constantly, and it does not care about your morning mantra.

So when you say "I am safe" while your shoulders are up around your ears and your jaw is clenched and your breath is shallow, your body hears a lie. It knows the truth. The truth lives in your posture, your breathing pattern, your muscle tension. Your body is not stupid. It's been protecting you for years. It's not going to override its survival programming because you read a quote on Instagram.

Think about it. If someone walked up to you with a knife and said "don't worry, I'm not going to hurt you," would you believe them? Of course not. You'd look at the knife. Your body would respond to the threat, not the words. Affirmations work the same way. Your body sees the mismatch between what you're saying and what you're feeling, and it chooses what it feels. Every single time.

The problem is not the affirmation. It's the gap.

Here's where things get interesting. The wellness industry sold us a lie. They told us that if we just thought positive enough, felt grateful enough, affirmed hard enough, we could think our way out of trauma. But Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," spent his career showing that trauma lives in the body, not the mind. You cannot talk your way out of a nervous system that has been wired for survival. You cannot affirm your way out of a body that learned to brace for impact before you could form sentences.

That gap between what you say and what you feel? That's not a failure. That's information. That's your body saying, "Hey, I hear you, but I don't trust you yet. You need to show me, not tell me." And this is where most people give up. They think the gap means they're doing it wrong. So they try harder. They repeat the affirmation louder. They buy another journal. They sign up for another course. And the gap gets wider.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office, sobbing, because I couldn't make myself believe I was worthy. I had all the tools. I had the worksheets. I had the meditation app. And none of it was working. She looked at me and said something I will never forget: "Your body doesn't need a new script. It needs a new experience." That sentence cracked something open in me. Because she was right. You can't convince a traumatized nervous system with words. You have to show it, over and over and over again, that it is safe now. And that takes time. And patience. And a willingness to move at the speed of the body, not the speed of your ambition.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). It breaks down how to actually work with the nervous system instead of against it. I wish I'd had it years ago.

Your nervous system has a memory. And it is long.

Here's the uncomfortable truth. Your body learned to survive in specific conditions. If you grew up in chaos, your nervous system learned that hypervigilance keeps you safe. If you were ignored, your nervous system learned that disappearing keeps you safe. If you were shamed, your nervous system learned that perfectionism keeps you safe. These are not choices. These are adaptations. And they are stored in the body like muscle memory.

So when you stand in front of the mirror and say "I am safe," your body checks its database. It scans through years of stored data. And it finds very little evidence that safety exists. It finds a lot of evidence that danger is everywhere. That people leave. That trust gets betrayed. That love comes with conditions. Your body is not trying to sabotage you. It's trying to protect you based on the data it has. And that data is old. But it's also real.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a physical imprint. A freezing of the nervous system that never got to complete its cycle. So when you try to override that with an affirmation, you're asking a frozen nervous system to thaw out because you said some words. It doesn't work that way. The body needs to move through the experience. It needs to discharge the energy. It needs to feel the feelings that got stuck.

And that's hard. That's really, really hard. Because the feelings are terrifying. The body knows that if it starts to thaw, it might feel everything it's been holding. And it's not sure it can survive that. So it holds tighter. It resists. It says "no, thank you, I'll stay frozen, that's safer." And then you feel like you're failing at healing. But you're not. You're just in the real work.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). It's Levine's own writing on how the body releases trauma. It's not an easy read. But it's a true one.

So what do you do instead? You work with the body, not against it.

Look, I'm not saying affirmations are useless. They can be helpful, but only after the nervous system has been regulated. Only after the body has some experience of safety. You can't put the roof on a house that doesn't have a foundation. The foundation is nervous system regulation. The roof is the cognitive work. Most of us are trying to put the roof on first.

Here's what actually works. Start with the body. Before you say a single word to yourself, check in. Where is your breath? Is it high in your chest or low in your belly? Where is your tension? Is your jaw tight? Are your shoulders up? Are your hands clenched? Don't try to change anything yet. Just notice. Just be curious. Your body has been trying to get your attention for years. Start by giving it some.

Then, do something physical that signals safety to the nervous system. Slow your exhale. Place a hand on your heart or your belly. Rock gently. Yawn. Sigh. These are not woo-woo exercises. These are biological interventions. They send signals up the vagus nerve to the brainstem that say, "Hey, we might be okay here." Deb Dana, who writes extensively on polyvagal theory, calls these "glimmers" - small moments of safety that we can cultivate intentionally.

Only after your body has some sense of safety, some sense of groundedness, do you introduce the affirmation. And even then, keep it small. Not "I am completely safe and whole and perfect." That's too big. Your body will reject it. Try something like "I am learning to feel safe in this moment." Or "I notice that I am breathing." Or even just "I am here." Your body can handle that. It's true. It's grounded. It's not asking your body to believe something it doesn't experience.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). It helped me understand why my body was saying no to my affirmations. It wasn't being difficult. It was being honest.

Healing is not about bypassing. It's about meeting yourself where you are.

I know you want to be better. I know you're tired of feeling this way. I know you've tried everything. And I know how much it hurts when the things that are supposed to help don't help. But please hear me on this. You are not behind. You are not failing. You are in the messy, slow, nonlinear process of teaching your body a new language. And that takes time. It takes repetition. It takes patience that feels impossible when you're suffering.

Affirmations are not bad. But they are not the first step. The first step is presence. The first step is noticing. The first step is letting your body know, through experience, not words, that it is safe to be here. That it is safe to feel. That it is safe to soften, just a little, just for now.

Your body does not believe your affirmations because your body is honest. It knows what it knows. It remembers what it remembers. And it will not be gaslit into pretending otherwise. So stop trying to convince it. Start listening to it. Start asking it what it needs. Start moving at its pace, not the pace of your healing timeline. That is the real work. And it is hard. And it is slow. And it is the only way through.

Stay with me here. You are not broken. Your nervous system is just doing its job. And it will learn, over time, that you are safe now. But it needs to feel it. Not just hear it.
